defs: Same as Arrack.

records.ety: [AS. hracca neck, hinder part of the head; cf. AS. hraca throat, G. rachen throat, E. retch.]

defs: The neck and spine of a fore quarter of veal or mutton.
A wreck; destruction.

records.ety: [Prob. fr. Icel. rek drift, motion, and akin to reka to drive, and E. wrack, wreck. √282.]

defs: Thin, flying, broken clouds, or any portion of floating vapor in the sky.
quotes: The winds in the upper region, which move the clouds above, which we call the rack, . . . pass without noise.
And the night rack came rolling up.

defs: To fly, as vapor or broken clouds.

records.ety: [See Rack that which stretches, or Rock, v.]
records.morph: [imp. & p. p. Racked (rkt); p. pr. & vb. n. Racking.]

defs: To amble fast, causing a rocking or swaying motion of the body; to pace; -- said of a horse.
A fast amble.

records.ety: [Cf. OF. vin raqu wine squeezed from the dregs of the grapes.]

defs: To draw off from the lees or sediment, as wine.
quotes: It is in common practice to draw wine or beer from the lees (which we call racking), whereby it will clarify much the sooner.

records.ety: [Probably fr. D. rek, rekbank, a rack, rekken to stretch; akin to G. reck, reckbank, a rack, recken to stretch, Dan. række, Sw. räcka, Icel. rekja to spread out, Goth. refrakjan to stretch out; cf. L. porrigere, Gr. 'ore`gein. √115. Cf. Right, a., Ratch.]

defs: An instrument or frame used for stretching, extending, retaining, or displaying, something.

An engine of torture, consisting of a large frame, upon which the body was gradually stretched until, sometimes, the joints were dislocated; -- formerly used judicially for extorting confessions from criminals or suspected persons.
An instrument for bending a bow.
A grate on which bacon is laid.
A frame or device of various construction for holding, and preventing the waste of, hay, grain, etc., supplied to beasts.
A frame on which articles are deposited for keeping or arranged for display; as, a clothes rack; a bottle rack, etc.
A piece or frame of wood, having several sheaves, through which the running rigging passes; -- called also rack block. Also, a frame to hold shot.
A frame or table on which ores are separated or washed.
A frame fitted to a wagon for carrying hay, straw, or grain on the stalk, or other bulky loads.
A distaff.
quotes: During the troubles of the fifteenth century, a rack was introduced into the Tower, and was occasionally used under the plea of political necessity.

defs: A bar with teeth on its face, or edge, to work with those of a wheel, pinion, or worm, which is to drive it or be driven by it.
That which is extorted; exaction.
quotes: A fit of the stone puts a king to the rack, and makes him as miserable as it does the meanest subject.

defs: To extend by the application of force; to stretch or strain; specifically, to stretch on the rack or wheel; to torture by an engine which strains the limbs and pulls the joints.
quotes: He was racked and miserably tormented.

defs: To torment; to torture; to affect with extreme pain or anguish.
quotes: Vaunting aloud but racked with deep despair.

defs: To stretch or strain, in a figurative sense; hence, to harass, or oppress by extortion.
quotes: The landlords there shamefully rack their tenants.
They [landlords] rack their rents an ace too high.
Grant that I may never rack a Scripture simile beyond the true intent thereof.
Try what my credit can in Venice do; That shall be racked even to the uttermost.

defs: To wash on a rack, as metals or ore.
To bind together, as two ropes, with cross turns of yarn, marline, etc.
